items.q: If you double your distance from a point source of waves, what happens to the intensity?
items.correct: It falls to one quarter — intensity follows an inverse-square law with distance from a point source.
items.misconception: It halves — intensity is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged because the wave doesn't lose energy.
items.why: Students default to linear scaling instead of the inverse-square law that arises because the same energy spreads over a sphere of growing surface area.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.
The answer examiners want is: Amps. “Volts” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Series. “Parallel” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a circuit has 2 cells of 1.5 V each in series, what is the total voltage in V?
items.why: The real figure is 3.0. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
